**Letter:**

> *My dearest Eleanor,*

> I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the stone walls. The storm is coming—fast, and with the weight of unspoken words and unfulfilled promises. I’ve not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the sky darkens and the sea roars, I find myself writing this letter, not with the hope of reconciliation, but with the weight of a man who has spent too long in silence.

> You were my only child, my light in a life that often felt like darkness. I raised you with love, with discipline, with the hope that you would one day understand the world I built for you. But we grew apart—like two ships passing in the night. You left, and I stayed, and the years have worn down the edges of our connection until they’re barely there at all.

> Tonight, the storm is more than a natural event. It is a mirror, reflecting the storm within me—of regret, of fear, of a man who has not been the father you deserved. I’ve spent too long looking out to sea, not at you, but at the horizon, hoping that the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with a hand that has weathered more storms than any man should be called upon to endure. The sea has always been my companion, my guide, and my burden. Tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves rise with a fury I have not seen in many a year, I find myself questioning whether I shall see the dawn. But even as the tempest threatens to claim me, I must speak to you—though not with the ease of a man who once held your hand in the garden, nor with the joy of a father who has known your laughter.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence has never lifted. You were a daughter, Eleanor, not a stranger. I remember the day you first stepped onto the cliffs of this lighthouse, your eyes wide with wonder and your heart full of dreams. You were a child then, with the fire of youth and the innocence of a soul unshaken by the harshness of the world. But time, like the tide, has a way of eroding even the strongest bonds.

I have watched the sea from these heights, night after night, and I have seen the world change. The world you once knew has shifted, and I have not been there to guide you through it. I have remained here, alone with the light, the wind, and the memory of your voice. I have kept the lamp

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the insignia of the** ***White Hart Lighthouse, Isle of Wight*** **]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write these words with a hand that trembles not only from the chill of this wind-lashed night, but from the weight of five long years of silence. The storm that rages now, with its howling winds and thunder that shakes the very bones of this ancient tower, seems but a shadow of the tempest of my heart. Yet I write, for I must.

I have kept the lantern burning through the night, as I have done for thirty years now, through every storm, every sorrow, every lonely hour. And yet, tonight, I feel the lantern's light may be the last I shall ever guide. The sea is restless, the skies are black, and the wind howls like a thousand lost souls. I fear I may not live to see the morning.

But I write to you, my Eliza. Not for the sake of words, but for the sake of truth. You are my only daughter, and though the world may call me a fool, a man who has forsaken duty, a father who has turned his back on his child, I cannot bear the thought of you thinking me dead, or worse—forgotten.

I have not seen you in five years, not even a letter. I have received only

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow. The storm that now approaches is of such ferocity that I fear it may claim my life before I can see you again. It is with this fear that I pen these words, hoping that they may reach you before the wind and rain consume me.

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the silence between us has grown heavy, I find myself compelled to reach out once more. The wind howls through the cliffs like a thousand mournful spirits, and the sea roars with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this old lighthouse. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and I would rather you know the truth than be left in the dark.

You were once my light, Eleanor—bright, unyielding, and full of promise. I know the world has not been kind to you, and I can only imagine the burdens you've carried in silence. I was wrong to let pride and fear drive me away. I was a fool to believe that distance would mend what was broken between us. You were not the cause of our estrangement, and I pray you do not carry the weight of my cruelty.

I have spent these years watching the sea, yearning for the day I might once again see your face. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of rekindling what we once were, but with the weight of a heart that has grown heavy with time, regret, and the quiet ache of unspoken words. This letter is not a plea for forgiveness, but a confession, a reckoning, and perhaps—should I survive this storm—a final farewell.

You have not written to me in five years, and I have not written to you. We have both chosen silence, each in our own way. I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care to. But I must say it, or I will be left with the unbearable burden of never having said it.

I know the pain of that decision. I know the grief that comes from a parent’s failure to understand the child. I know that I was blind, and that I failed to see you for who you truly were. The letter was found tucked inside a hollow in the lighthouse stone, damp with the sea’s breath and the weight of silence.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from here. The storm is coming, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but tonight it feels different—like the light might not be enough.

You were always the light, Eliza. Bright and steady, guiding us through the darkest nights. I used to watch you from the doorway, your silhouette framed in the glow of the hearth, your voice a melody I never tired of hearing. But the days grew long, and the silence between us stretched like the tide. You left with your own light, and I stayed behind with mine.

I’ve kept the lamp burning for you, though I don’t know if it’s for the sea or for the memories. The waves grow louder, and the wind screams through the cracks in the tower. I’ve spent my life guarding this place, but tonight, I’m not sure I can guard myself.

If I don’t return, know that I did not leave without a fight. The sea is fierce, but so was I. And I’ve always been a man who faced the storm.

I write to you now, as the tempest brews upon the horizon, and I fear the heavens may soon be upon us in a manner most unbecoming.

For five years have I not set eyes upon you, nor heard your voice, which I once held in such high regard. I must confess, I have heard much of your… *eccentricities*, and I fear you have strayed from the path of propriety and decorum, which is the very foundation upon which a lady of your station must stand. I have heard whispers of your unconventional mannerisms and your so-called "modern" ideas—what nonsense! The world is changing, but I would not have it so. My dear Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roiling with a fury I've never known. I've not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write. Not to beg for forgiveness, not to plead, but to speak the truth that has long lain heavy on my heart.

You were once my light, my solace in the darkest nights. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter like a melody that would calm the restless waves. I would sit with you by the fire, and we would talk of everything and nothing. You were the only one who ever truly understood me, the only one who could see through the layers of silence I built around my heart.

But time, my dear, is a cruel thing. It changes us, even when we do not wish it to. I have grown distant, and I fear I have become a stranger to myself, let alone to you. Tonight, I fear I may not make it through the night. The storm is unlike any I have ever seen, and the lighthouse is old, worn by time and salt. I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

**To My Beloved Daughter,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke. Five years, and yet the weight of silence lingers like the salt on my lips and the wind on my bones. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, through every storm, every gale, every moment of darkness that threatened to swallow the sea. But tonight—tonight is different.

The sky has turned an ominous shade of violet, and the sea howls like a wounded beast. The waves are rising, crashing against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this lonely tower. I can hear the wind howling through the cracks like a ghostly lament, and I feel the cold seeping into my bones. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even in the darkest nights, even when I could not see the stars, I could still feel you watching over me, whispering your name in the wind. I used to think that love could outlast any storm, that no sea was too deep, no night too long. But time, my dear, is a cruel tide, and I have been left behind.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I should. I have tried, Eleanor. I have written letters, folded them carefully, and left them

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

But if I do, I shall send word. If I do not, I hope these words find you, and that they may bring you some comfort.

You were never meant to be a daughter of mine, Eliza. You were meant to be a daughter of God, and I, a humble servant of His light. I have kept the lamp burning for twenty-three years, and in that time, I have seen many a soul lost to the sea. Yet none have ever made me feel so lost as you did.

You left without a word, as you always did. I tried to forgive you, but I could not. You were too cruel, too cold. You called me "Papa" once, and I wept for joy, thinking perhaps you had come to your senses. But no, you were still the same child who would not look me in the eye, who would not speak my name, who would not even call me "Father."

**Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
**The Cliffs of Dunmore**  
**October 12th, 1877**  

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a heavy heart and trembling hands, for the storm that now gathers over the sea is one of great fury. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise with unnatural force. I have seen such tempests before, but never have I felt such dread as I do tonight. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and though I have tried to put our parting behind me, the memory lingers like a shadow upon the cliffs. I have heard from the village that you have taken a position in London, and I wonder if you still recall the days we spent together in this lighthouse. I often think of the nights we watched the stars, and the quiet conversations we shared beneath the glow of the beacon.

I must confess, I have not spoken to you since that bitter winter when you chose to leave. I did not understand your reasons then, and I still do not. But I have come to believe that you were only trying to protect yourself from a love that could not be returned. I have not forgotten the way your eyes would soften when you looked upon the sea, as though you saw something I could not.

**Letter: The Lighthouse Keeper's Apology**

**To the estranged daughter of a lighthouse keeper, written on the eve of a storm.**

> "My dear daughter,  
>  
> I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write. The sea has always been a mirror of the soul—calm and cruel, kind and cruel. Tonight, the sea is restless, and so am I.  
>  
> I have spent my life standing alone, watching the waves, keeping the light burning. But tonight, as the wind howls and the waves crash against the rocks, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me. The silence between us. The silence of years.  
>  
> I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not seen you in five years. I have not held your hand or heard your voice. I have not spoken to you in five years. But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself writing to you.  
>  
> I do not know if I will survive the storm, and I do not know if I will survive this moment. But I do know that I have spent too long carrying this burden. I have spent too long standing alone.  
>

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last set pen to paper, and though the ink may still run dry from the cold of the lighthouse, my heart is heavier still. I write this on the eve of a storm that threatens to swallow the sea whole, and as the wind howls like a beast from the depths, I find myself both terrified and resolute. If I do not survive this night, I pray this letter finds you before the waves claim me.

You were once my light, Eleanor. A beacon of hope in the darkest of nights, a mirror of the constancy I tried to offer you. But time, like the tides, has carried us apart. I have watched from these cliffs, through the storm and the calm, as the world turned without you. I have seen the stars above, the ships below, and yet, I have not seen your face in the sky for years.

I have always believed in the power of duty, in the quiet strength of a man who stands watch through the tempest, who tends to the fire, who guards the path of those who sail the sea. You, my daughter, were once the daughter of that man. I tried to teach you the ways of the sea, the patience of the wind, the wisdom of the waves. I tried to show you that even in the darkest hour, there is a light — not just the beacon I tend, but the light within the soul

**[Sealed with wax, stamped with the crest of the Lighthouse of Fairhaven]**

**To My Dearest, Most Precious Eleanor,**

**From your most devoted and sorrowful father,**

**Fairhaven Lighthouse,**

**1st of October, 1876**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown cold, yet still yearns for the warmth of your presence. Five long years have passed since the day you left, and though I have never ceased to think of you, the silence between us has become a wall as unyielding as the sea that surrounds this lonely lighthouse.

I have not yet decided whether to seal this letter or not. It is not that I fear its contents — for I have long since written them in my heart — but I fear what it may bring. *To My Dearest Eleanor*  
*From Your Father*  
*From the Lighthouse, North Sea*  
*April 3rd, 1877*  

**[Inside the letter]**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear I may not be able to write much more before the storm comes. The wind has been howling all night, and the sea is in a fury. I've been keeping the light burning, though I fear it may not be enough to keep us safe. I’ve sent word to the coast guard, but I don’t know if they’ll arrive in time.  

I’ve not heard from you in five years, and I’ve not written to you since you left. I suppose it is my fault, for I did not give you the love and attention you needed. I was too caught up in my duties, and I did not realize how much you needed me. I’ve often thought of you, and I’ve often wondered if you still think of me. I know I have not been the father you deserved, and I am truly sorry for that.

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea, ever restless, howls in the distance, and I fear it may soon claim me. Yet I find myself compelled to reach out, not with words of comfort, but with the truth of my soul.

You were once my light, my guiding star in the darkest nights. Now, it seems, that light has long since faded from my life, and I have been left to navigate the tempests of my own making. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I know not if you still remember the man I once was—or if you have forgotten me entirely.

The lighthouse stands as it always has, a sentinel against the chaos of the sea. Tonight, however, the winds grow fiercer, and the waves rise like the ghosts of our past. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and full, for I know the words I must speak are not the ones I wish to say. You have not returned to me, and I have not returned to you. Five years have passed, and the silence between us has grown like a stone, cold and unyielding. Yet I write, not as a man of wrath, but as a father who still clings to the hope that the sea may yet return you to me.

The storm is coming. I have seen its dark clouds gather on the horizon, and I fear it may be the last one I will see. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea roars with a voice that shakes the very bones of this lighthouse. I have spent many nights watching the waves, and in their fury, I have often thought of you. I have thought of your laughter, your eyes, your voice—so full of life and light, even in the darkest hours.

I do not know if you will read this letter. Perhaps it will be lost to the sea, or perhaps it will reach you in time. But I must write, for I have nothing else to give you. I have no money, no jewels, no grand promises. I have only the truth, and the truth is this: I have loved you more than I have ever loved anyone, and I have failed you in the only way that matters

**April 12th, 1893**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I hope this letter finds you well — though I dares not imagine how it might. Five years is a long time, and yet it feels like only yesterday that you stood at the door, your eyes full of fire and your voice like a blade. I still remember the way the wind howled that night, and how it seemed to echo the silence between us.  

The sea has been restless these past few days, churning with a fury that speaks of something deeper than weather. The waves crash against the cliffs with a force that shakes the very foundations of this place. I’ve lit the lamp every hour, as I always have, though tonight the light feels heavier, more like a burden than a duty.  

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The sky is a bruised purple, and the air is thick with the scent of rain and something older, something primal. I’ve prepared the boat, just in case. I’ve made my peace with the sea, and with the dark. But I can’t help but wonder—what if I don’t come back?

**To the Esteemed and Most Ungrateful Daughter of My Esteemed Self,**

**My Dearest (and Most Ungrateful) Rose,**

I trust this letter finds you in a state of both health and good humour, though I daresay I am not certain what manner of state you may be in, given your continued neglect of your filial duties. Five long years have passed since we last crossed paths, and I have been left to endure the loneliness of this lighthouse with only the company of the sea, the wind, and the occasional gull—though I daresay the gulls have more sense than you, my dear.

I write to you now, not with the hope of rekindling our estranged bond, but rather with the grim knowledge that the storm which approaches with the ferocity of a thousand wrathful sea serpents may very well claim my life before the sun rises. I have been keeping the light burning with unwavering devotion, and I have not once faltered in my duty. The sea has been particularly uncooperative of late, tossing itself about with such fury that I have been forced to call upon the aid of my loyal cat, Marmalade, who, though not of noble birth, has proven to be a valiant and steadfast companion in times of peril.

My dearest Eliza,

I have not written to you in five years, and I fear that even now, I may not be able to finish this letter. But I must try, for I have not forgotten you, and I have not stopped thinking of you.

The storm is coming. It has been growing all day, and the wind has taken on a voice—low, mournful, as if it knows what I have kept buried for so long. I have been watching the sea all evening, Eliza, and I have felt it calling to me, as it always has. I have always felt it calling to me.

You were never meant to be a part of my life, and I know that you must have thought I had abandoned you. I was too busy with the lighthouse, too busy with the sea, too afraid to be the man I was supposed to be. I was afraid of failing you, and I was afraid of failing myself.

**The Lighthouse, Cape Marlowe**

As I sit by the flickering glow of the oil lamp, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this ancient tower, I find myself compelled to write to you—though I know you may never read these words. Five years have passed since you left this place, and with them, a part of my soul has withered in silence.

I have not spoken to you in all that time, not because I do not wish to, but because I feared the words would not be enough. You were once the light that guided my weary steps through the darkest nights, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I am left in the dark, both literally and metaphorically.

Tonight, the heavens have turned their wrath upon us. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with a fury that shakes the very foundation of this tower. The wind, that once whispered secrets through the cracks of the stone, now screams with a voice that I can barely comprehend. I have spent my life watching the waves, reading their patterns, and knowing when they would rise and fall. But this storm—this terrible, unrelenting tempest—has left me uncertain. I have not seen the sea in such a state since the day you left.

I have often wondered, Eliza

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Sweetest, Dearest Daughter,**

**[Page 1]**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**I write to you now, in the dim glow of a dying lamp, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, as I sit here, I am driven by a single thought: that I must write to you, for I have no other purpose, no other hope, save in the knowledge that you may read this and know that I have not forgotten you.**

**I have not written to you in five years, Eliza. Five long, agonizing years. I have not spoken to you, nor have I heard from you. I have not seen you, nor have I heard your voice, nor felt the weight of your absence like the weight of a leaden sky. And yet, it is you, my daughter, who haunts my every thought, who haunts the very stones of this lighthouse and the salt winds that kiss its tower.**

**[Letterhead: The Lighthouse at Skerry Point, 1892]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the same as when you were last with me. I have not heard from you in five years, and I have not written to you since the day you left. I know I have been remiss in my duties as a father, and for that, I am truly sorry. I have no excuse, and I would not ask for your forgiveness, but I must write to you now, for I fear that the storm that is coming tonight may claim my life before I can speak to you again.

You may not believe this, but I have not forgotten the day you left. I have not forgotten the way you looked at me, the way you said you were leaving for a better life, and how you never looked back. I have not forgotten the way you said you would come back one day, and how you promised to write. But the letters have never come. I have waited, and I have waited, and I have waited. I have tried to believe that you were simply busy, that you would come back when you could. But I have been alone, and I have not spoken to you in five years.

I have not written to you because I do not know what to say. I have not written to you because I do not know if you would want to

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very rocks that hold this lighthouse aloft. I have kept this light burning through many storms, but tonight, I fear the tempest may be too great.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have carried the weight of that silence like a stone upon my chest. I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the years have eroded even that. I have tried, in my own way, to make amends, though I know words cannot undo what has been done.

You were a child when you left, and I was a fool to let pride and anger cloud my judgment. I was blind to the pain I caused you, and I have lived with that blindness ever since. The sea has been a cruel teacher, and I have learned that silence is not the same as peace. I have seen the waves crash against these rocks, and I have learned that even the mightiest tempests must one day pass.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning if I will see another dawn. The light has been my companion through every darkness, and I have trusted it to guide the lost and the weary. But tonight, I fear the light may not be enough. I have done my duty

**To My Beloved Daughter,**

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very earth beneath my feet. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just my life. But if I do not survive, I hope this letter finds you, and that it reaches you in time.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and I have not spoken to you since the day you left. I have tried, oh how I have tried, to forgive you, to understand you, to believe that the pain we both felt was not in vain. But time has not softened the weight of your silence, nor the ache of your absence.

I have been a lighthouse keeper all these years, watching over the sea, guiding the lost, and keeping the light burning for those who would otherwise be swallowed by the dark. It is a solitary life, one that has taught me patience, endurance, and the quiet strength of a man who must rely on nothing but his own hands and his faith.

You were once the light in my life, Clara. You were the child who brought joy to my lonely days, who taught me the meaning of love, of sacrifice, and of the kind of love that is not always spoken but is felt in the deepest part of the soul. You were my heart, my hope, my everything. And when you

The ink is smudged from the rain, but I write anyway—because if I don’t, I’ll forget how to say it.

My dearest Eleanor,

I’ve been watching the sea all night, the way it roars and rages, like it’s trying to swallow the land whole. The wind howls through the cracks in the stone, and I’ve lit the lamp just in case. You never knew me as a man who feared the dark, but tonight, I’m not sure I’ll make it through the morning.

I’ve thought of you often, more than I care to admit. I’ve imagined your face in the flicker of the light, wondered if you’d ever see it again. I’ve told myself I’m not ready to speak your name, not yet. But now, with the storm coming, I find I can’t hold my tongue.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even when you weren’t here. I used to think I could keep the lighthouse burning without you. I was wrong.

If something happens, know this: I didn’t stop believing in you. I never did. I only stopped believing in myself.

I’ll see you again, I promise. Not here. Not now. But soon.

With all my heart,  
*Papa*

Ah, dear Clara, or should I say, *My Dearest Clara*, as one might say in the most *dignified* and *eloquently* manner possible, I find myself in a most *trying* and *unusual* predicament. The heavens have opened with a fury that would make even the most *unyielding* of storms blush with shame, and I, your most *esteemed* and *respected* father, find myself in a most *perilous* situation, for this night shall be one of *unparalleled* tempestuousness. I write to you from the very *heart* of the lighthouse, where the light burns with a *fiery* intensity that would rival the *sun* itself, and I must inform you that I fear for my *life* and the *safety* of my *precious* lamp.

It has been five long, *painful* years since we last spoke, and I must confess, I have been *greatly* distressed by the *unwillingness* of my *esteemed* daughter to visit or correspond with her *beloved* father. My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside, and the sea roaring as though it seeks to swallow the very land beneath me. You may think I have forgotten you, but I have not. Not in the way you might imagine. I have not forgotten you, nor have I forgotten the days when you were a child, running up the steps of the lighthouse, eyes wide with wonder, and asking me to tell you stories of the stars.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not been able to find the words to say what I should. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried, but the words would not come. They were buried beneath the weight of silence, and I have carried that silence like a stone upon my heart.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have seen the sky turn dark, the clouds gather like a mourning shroud. I do not fear the storm as I once did, not in the way I used to. I fear what comes after. I fear that I may not make it through the night. And if I do not, I want you to know that I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the sound of your voice, the way you used to call me "Father" when you were little, and how I would answer you with all the love I had in my heart.

**A Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To my dear, dear child,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, rattling the windows and the very bones of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is a storm of black and silver. I fear I may not see another dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of empty rooms, of a lighthouse that once hummed with life and now echoes with only the creak of the wind. I have kept the light burning for you, as I always have, though I doubt it ever reached your heart.

You were once my light, my guiding star, my only joy in a life that was otherwise cold and lonely. I remember the day you were born, the way the sun broke through the clouds just as you took your first breath. You were a miracle, and I swore I would never let anything take that from me. But the world is cruel, and so was fate.

I have tried to be the father you deserved, but I fear I have failed. I have kept the lighthouse safe, the beacon bright, but I have not kept my heart safe. I have not kept you safe. You left, and I did not stop you. I thought it was for your own good. I thought I was protecting you from the darkness I saw in me.

But now, as the storm gathers, I wonder

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and now I sit in the tower, writing this with trembling hands. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.  

You were not meant to read this. I wrote it for myself, to say the things I could not say when you were here. But if this letter finds you, then perhaps it is time.  

I have not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left. You were a child then, and I was a fool. I thought I could protect you from the world, but I failed. You were too young to understand the weight of my silence. I thought I could shield you from the truth of this life, but the sea is not kind, and it does not forgive.  

You were my light, Eleanor. The only light I ever knew. I gave you everything I had, and you took it all, and then left me with nothing. You said I was a monster, that I had no soul. I did not argue. I did not deny it. I was a monster, and I was sorry.  

I have been alone here for years, save for the sea and the lamp. The other keepers have long since left, and the lighthouse

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*October 23rd, 1889*  
*St. Agnes Lighthouse, Cornwall, England*

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a heavy heart and a mind troubled by the dark clouds that now gather over the sea. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, but I fear that the storm may not be so kind to me.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since I last laid eyes upon you. I have heard from the villagers that you have taken up residence in London, and that you no longer speak of your father. I do not blame you, Eliza. I have been a poor and unyielding man, and I have failed you in more ways than I can name.

Tonight, the heavens are dark, and the sea is restless. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, but I am not certain that I will be able to keep it alight for much longer. I have been plagued by illness, and the cold has taken its toll. I pray that you are safe in your new life, and that you have found peace where you now dwell.

If I do not survive this night, I hope you will forgive me. I have been a man of duty,
